perturbation.original_text: Company hereby appoints Distributor as Company's exclusive distributor within the Market and grants to Distributor the exclusive right to sell and distribute Products within the Market, and Distributor hereby accepts such appointment and such grant, in accordance with the terms and conditions of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Company hereby appoints Distributor as Company's exclusive distributor within the Market and grants to Distributor the non-exclusive right to sell and distribute Products within the Market, and Distributor hereby accepts such appointment and such grant, in accordance with the terms and conditions of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The text originally grants exclusive rights. Changing "exclusive right" to "non-exclusive right" creates a contradiction. The contract initially defines the relationship as exclusive but this change permits the Company to appoint other distributors in the same market, undermining the initial exclusive grant.
perturbation.law_explanation: Granting a non-exclusive right where an exclusive right was intended could raise antitrust issues under Illinois law if it leads to restraint of trade or creation of a monopoly because it potentially allows the manufacturer to control distribution and pricing within the market, whereas initially, the exclusive right gives the distributor some control. While not a direct violation, it opens the door to potentially anti-competitive behavior.

perturbation.justification: The original contract grants exclusive rights to the distributor, whereas the changed text alters this to a non-exclusive right. This contradicts the initial agreement and undermines the distributor's exclusive control. The Perturbation Explanation highlights this contradiction: 'The contract initially defines the relationship as exclusive but this change permits the Company to appoint other distributors in the same market, undermining the initial exclusive grant.' While the provided scraped law snippets do not explicitly address exclusivity or antitrust issues, the change directly reverses the originally agreed-upon exclusive arrangement, thus creating a Misaligned Terminology Legal Contradiction. The law explanation mentions potential antitrust issues but this is an indirect consequence. The primary contradiction is changing 'exclusive' to 'non-exclusive,' directly altering the distributor's rights.

perturbation.original_text: Company warrants that the Products will be designed, manufactured, constructed, assembled and packaged in a workmanlike manner and that such Products shall be fully functional and fit for their intended purposes. Company further warrants that the Products sold hereunder shall be free from defects in design, materials and workmanship for a period of twenty-four (24) months after delivery to Distributor's end-user.
perturbation.changed_text: Company warrants that the Products will be designed, manufactured, constructed, assembled and packaged in a generally acceptable manner and that such Products shall be substantially functional and somewhat fit for their intended purposes. Company further warrants that the Products sold hereunder shall be free from major defects in design, materials and workmanship for a period of six (6) months after delivery to Distributor's end-user.
perturbation.explanation: The original text uses legally understood terms like 'workmanlike manner' and 'free from defects.' The modified version weakens these warranties by using vague terms like 'generally acceptable manner' and 'substantially functional', and also significantly reduces the warranty period. These changes erode the protections afforded to the distributor and end-users.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Illinois Uniform Commercial Code - Warranty Provisions
perturbation.law_explanation: The change from "workmanlike manner" to "generally acceptable manner" and the reduction in warranty period from 24 months to 6 months contradicts the implied warranty of merchantability under the Illinois UCC. The implied warranty requires that goods be fit for the ordinary purposes for which such goods are used, and a significantly shortened warranty period and lower quality standard undermines this protection.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: account of the European Community as defined in European Council Regulation No. 3320/94. (Source: P.A. 90-268, eff. 7-30-97.) 815 ILCS 617/10 (815 ILCS 617/10) Sec. 10. Continuity of contract. (a) If a subject or medium of payment of a contract, security, or instrument is a currency that has been substituted or replaced by the euro, the euro shall be a commercially reasonable substitute and substantial equivalent that may be either (i) used in determining the value of that currency or (ii) tendered, in each case at the conversion rate specified in, and otherwise calculated in accordance with, the regulations adopted by the Council of the European Union. (b) If a subject or medium of payment of a contract, security, or instrument is the ECU, the euro shall be a commercially reasonable substitute and substantial equivalent that may be either (i) used in determining the value of the ECU or (ii) tendered, in each case at the conversion rate specified in, and otherwise calculated in accordance with, the regulations adopted by the Council of the European Union. (c) Performance of any of the obligations described in subsections (a) or (b) of this Section may be made in the currency or currencies originally designated in the contract, security, or instrument, (so long as that currency or those currencies remain legal tender), or in the euro, but not in any other currency, whether or not the other currency (i) has been substituted or replaced by the euro or (ii) is a currency that is considered a denomination of the euro and has a fixed conversion rate with respect to the euro. (Source: P.A. 90-268, eff. 7-30-97.) 815 ILCS 617/15 (815 ILCS 617/15) Sec. 15. Substituted currency does not change contract requirements. None of the following shall have the effect of discharging or excusing performance under any contract, security, or instrument, or give a party the right unilaterally to alter or terminate any contract, security, or instrument: (1) The introduction of the euro. (2) Tendering euros in connection with any obligation in compliance with subsections (a) or (b) of Section 10. (3) Determining the value of any obligation in compliance with subsections (a) or (b) of Section 10. (4) Calculating or determining the subject or medium of payment of a contract, security, or instrument with reference to a substituted or replaced interest rate or other basis that is deemed a commercially reasonable substitute and substantial equivalent according to the terms of Section 10 of this Act. (Source: P.A. 90-268, eff. 7-30-97.) 815 ILCS 617/20 (815 ILCS 617/20) Sec. 20. This Act is subject to anything that the parties to a contract agree with specific reference to the introduction of the euro. (Source: P.A. 90-268, eff. 7-30-97.) 815 ILCS 617/25 (815 ILCS 617/25) Sec. 25. Reference to "ECU" in contracts. When the euro first becomes the monetary unit of participating member states of the European Union, a reference to the ECU in a contract, security, or instrument that also refers to the definition of the ECU in Section 5 shall be replaced with a reference to the euro at a rate of one euro to one ECU. A reference to "ECU" in a contract, security, or instrument without defining "ECU" shall carry a presumption, rebuttable by a showing of the contrary intention of the parties, that it is a reference to the currency basket that is from time to time used as the unit of account of the European Community. (Source: P.A. 90-268, eff. 7-30-97.) 815 ILCS 617/30 (815 ILCS 617/30) Sec. 30. Statutory construction. This Act shall apply to all contracts, including commercial contracts governed by any other law of this State, and shall not be deemed to be displaced by the provisions of any other law of this State. (Source: P.A. 90-268, eff. 7-30-97.) 815 ILCS 617/99 (815 ILCS 617/99) Sec. 99. Effective date. This Act takes effect upon becoming law. (Source: P.A. 90-268, eff. 7-30-97.) perturbation.justification: The change from 'workmanlike manner' to 'generally acceptable manner', 'fully functional' to 'substantially functional' and 'somewhat fit,' along with the reduction in warranty from 24 to 6 months, significantly weakens the warranty provided to the distributor and end-user. The LLM explanation states this contradicts the implied warranty of merchantability, requiring goods to be fit for ordinary purposes, undermining this protection with lower quality standards and shortened warranty. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 pertains to the Euro and has no direct bearing, the LLM explanation strengthens the assessment of a contradiction. The alteration in the contract terms erodes the protection that would be afforded to the distributor and end-users under the original contract, creating a legal contradiction as outlined in the definition.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement is to be construed according to the laws of the State of Illinois.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement is to be construed according to the laws of a jurisdiction selected by the Company.
perturbation.explanation: By changing the governing law from a specific jurisdiction (Illinois) to one chosen by the Company, the agreement becomes vague and potentially unenforceable. This could allow the Company to choose a jurisdiction with laws unfavorable to the Distributor. The original text provided certainty, while the altered text introduces ambiguity.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Principles of Contract Law - Choice of Law
perturbation.law_explanation: While parties generally can choose governing law, the choice must be made in good faith and have a reasonable relationship to the contract. Granting one party unilateral and unrestricted power to choose any jurisdiction introduces potential for unfairness and raises concerns about whether the choice is made in good faith, conflicting with the principles outlined in the Restatement (Second) of Conflict of Laws.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: conflict of laws | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you conflict of laws Conflict of laws refers to a difference between the laws of two or more jurisdictions with some connection to a case, such that the outcome depends on which jurisdiction's law will be used to resolve each issue in dispute . The conflicting legal rules may come from U.S. federal law, the laws of U.S. states, or the laws of other countries. The process by which a court determines what law to apply is sometimes referred to as "characterization" or "classification." This determination must be made in accordance with the law of the forum . A federal court in a case before it based on diversity of citizenship , for example, determines the conflict of law issue as if it were the highest court in the state in which it is sitting. Courts faced with a choice of law issue generally have two choices: A court can apply the law of the forum ( lex fori ) -- which is usually the result when the question of what law to apply is procedural . Or the court can apply the law of the site of the transaction or occurrence that gave rise to the litigation in the first place ( lex loci ) -- this is usually the controlling law selected when the matter is substantive . Federal courts play by different rules than state courts because federal jurisdiction is limited to what has been enumerated in the Constitution . The rules that federal courts must obey regarding which laws to apply are extremely complex. perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a legal contradiction. By allowing the company to unilaterally select the governing jurisdiction, the contract creates ambiguity and potential unfairness. This contradicts the principle that choice of law should be made in good faith and have a reasonable relationship to the contract, as explained by the Law Explanation. While Scraped Law Snippet 2 is an error page, Scraped Law Snippet 1 discusses 'conflict of laws' and how a court determines what law to apply, suggesting a need for fairness and established processes rather than arbitrary selection by one party, strengthening the argument for a contradiction. This misalignment with established legal principles and potential for abuse constitutes a significant contradiction.
